title: Adding Transcript Review to Your Client Onboarding
description: How to make IRS transcript analysis a standard part of every new client intake — and why it protects your practice and your clients.
content:
Why Transcript Review Belongs in Onboarding

When a new client walks in, you don't know what's on their IRS account. Surprises — unfiled years, existing balances, open audits — discovered mid-engagement are expensive and disruptive. A transcript review at intake eliminates surprises before they become your problem.

What You Learn from an Onboarding Transcript Review

  • Whether all returns have been filed (no missing years)
  • Existing balances, penalties, and interest
  • Open audits or examinations (code 420, 424)
  • Refund holds or freezes (code 570, 810)
  • Prior transcript access by other practitioners

How to Position It to Clients

Frame the transcript review as due diligence — the same way a doctor reviews a patient's medical history before treatment. Tell clients: 'Before we can advise you, we need to see your complete IRS account history. This takes about 10 minutes and protects both of us.'

Billing for Onboarding Transcript Review

You have two options: include transcript review in your onboarding fee, or charge it separately as a $99–$149 add-on. Charging separately signals its value and creates an explicit deliverable — the client receives a report they can keep.

Workflow Integration

  1. Add transcript request to your intake form (Form 4506-C or IRS online access)
  2. Run transcript through parser during or immediately after intake meeting
  3. Review report before the first advisory session
  4. Present findings to client as part of your onboarding summary
  5. Use findings to scope the full engagement
